blocks.text: Chapter 2.
Keeping Score: Foxes, Hedgehogs, and the Tournament
Second night, friend — and it begins with a spy story about four little words that meant everything and nothing.

1951. The Cold War is young and terrifying. America's National Intelligence Estimate — the most important document in the intelligence world — judges that a Soviet attack on Yugoslavia is a "serious possibility." Grave men in Washington read those words and made decisions about war.

Then Sherman Kent — the father of modern intelligence analysis — did something no one had thought to do. He asked the very analysts who had signed that estimate a simple question: what does "serious possibility" actually mean, in numbers?
The poets of the apocalypse

Pause and guess: Same four words. Same document. Same analysts who had all signed off on it. When Kent made each one privately translate "serious possibility" into a percentage — how far apart were they?
Ready? Here is what happened.

From roughly 20% to 80%. The same phrase, in the same sentence, meant near-certain war to one signatory and probably nothing to another — and none of them had known they disagreed. Kent proposed the obvious fix: a standard schedule mapping words to numbers — "almost certain" ≈ 93%, "probable" ≈ 75%. His colleagues rejected it as unseemly — numbers made analysts sound like bookies. Kent's immortal retort: better a bookie than a poet. The poets won, friend — and their culture of comfortable vagueness still rules punditry today, because vagueness is not sloppiness. It is insurance. "China's rise will falter" — when? "A recession is possible" — how possible? Elastic words fit any outcome, and the forecaster grades his own homework afterward, and always passes. A judgeable forecast needs three things: a precisely defined event, a hard deadline, and a number. And one subtlety to carry forever: a single 70% call that fails isn't disproven — maybe the 30% happened. Skill only shows across many scored forecasts. Which is why forecasting had to become a tournament.

And once forecasts are numbers, accuracy is computable: the Brier score — the gap between your probabilities and reality, squared and summed. Zero is omniscience; 0.5 is mindless coin-flipping; 2.0 is perfect wrongness. It rewards two virtues at once: calibration — your 60% calls come true about 60% of the time — and resolution — the courage to leave the safety of "maybe." A perpetual fence-sitter can be perfectly calibrated and perfectly useless. And every score needs a benchmark, friend: the Phoenix weathercaster scores near-zero forecasting sunshine forever; the same score in changeable weather would be genius. Same questions, same deadlines, head to head — that's a tournament.
The fox and the hedgehog

Tetlock ran the first great one: 284 experts, twenty years, ~28,000 scored predictions. You know the chimp headline. Now meet the real discovery — the two kinds of minds, named from Isaiah Berlin's essay: the fox knows many things, but the hedgehog knows one big thing.

Hedgehogs know One Big Thing — a governing idea (any ideology will do) — and stretch it to explain everything. They pile up reasons they're right, radiate confidence, love the words moreover and certain. Their forecasting record: dismal — below chance at long horizons, and worst inside their own specialty, where the Big Idea gripped hardest. Foxes know many little things: they aggregate sources, speak in however and on the other hand, hold beliefs as hypotheses, tolerate ambiguity. The foxes beat chance, beat the hedgehogs, and were far better calibrated — real, if modest, foresight.
Pause and guess: Then Tetlock checked one more variable against accuracy: how often each expert appeared in the media. What was the relationship between fame and forecasting skill?
Ready? Here is what happened.

Inverse. The more television an expert did, the worse their predictions — the same perverse pattern Taleb found with confident pundits. And the mechanism is pure supply and demand: audiences crave simple, confident stories, and hedgehogs deliver them beautifully. The qualities that make a great talking head — certainty, one clean narrative, no "on the other hand" — are precisely the qualities that ruin forecasts. The market for expertise rewards the wrong thing... because nobody keeps score. So audit yourself, friend: if one Big Idea explains all your predictions, and you can't remember the last time you said "however" — deliberately gather the sources that cut against you. And when choosing whose forecasts to trust, discount fame entirely; ask one question only: where is the scored track record?
The amateurs versus the analysts

In 2002, the U.S. intelligence community — twenty thousand analysts, a budget north of $50 billion — judged it a near-certainty that Iraq held weapons of mass destruction. It didn't. And Tetlock's autopsy is precise, friend: the sin wasn't being wrong — probabilistic calls sometimes lose. The sin was saying ~100% when an honest process might have said 60-70% — a difference that could have changed a war debate.

One response was IARPA — the intelligence world's research agency — which in 2011 launched a genuine forecasting tournament: five university teams, four years, ~500 real geopolitical questions — will Greece leave the eurozone? will North Korea test a device by this date? — every forecast scored by Brier, head to head. Tetlock and colleagues entered the Good Judgment Project: thousands of ordinary volunteers recruited online. Retirees. Programmers. Homemakers. Armed with Google and a scoreboard.

The results were a rout. GJP beat the official control group by 60% in year one, 78% in year two, and crushed the rival university teams by 30-70% — so decisively that IARPA dropped the other teams after two years. And by press accounts, GJP's best volunteers outperformed professional intelligence analysts with access to classified information by roughly 30% on the same questions. Read that again, friend: amateurs at kitchen tables, kept honest by scoring, out-forecast the professionals with the secrets. Measurement beat clearance.
Skill, not luck

GJP's top 2% each year were dubbed superforecasters — people like Doug Lorch, a retired IBM programmer whose Brier score of 0.14 made him the best of thousands. The obvious objection: flip enough coins and someone gets ten heads. Lucky tail?

The data says no. If luck ruled, the top performers should crash back toward average the next year — heavy regression to the mean. Instead, year-to-year accuracy correlated at about 0.65, and roughly 70% of superforecasters stayed in the top 2% the following year — against the ~2% chance would predict. Two more nails: grouped into elite teams, their edge widened rather than evaporated. And the size of the edge is the astonishing part — superforecasters looking 300 days ahead were more accurate than regular forecasters looking just 100 days ahead. They could see three times farther through the fog.

So foresight is real, measurable, and unevenly distributed — and the people who have it aren't the celebrities on your screen. They are disciplined, ordinary-looking thinkers using learnable methods. Which raises the only question that matters, friend: what exactly do they do?
Before you go

So: vague forecasts are insurance policies, not predictions — demand the event, the deadline, and the number; the Brier score weds calibration to courage; foxes beat hedgehogs while fame predicts failure; kitchen-table volunteers beat the classified professionals; and the top 2% stay on top — it's skill.

Tomorrow, friend, we open the toolbox. When a superforecaster meets an impossible-seeming question — "will polonium be found in Arafat's body?" — they don't google harder. They take the question apart like a watchmaker: Fermi-style decomposition, the outside view before the inside view, and the strange arithmetic that starts every great forecast with someone else's number. Next time: the watchmaker's method — how superforecasters actually think.
Key takeaways
"Serious possibility" meant 20% to one signer and 80% to another — vague words are insurance, not forecasts; demand event, deadline, number.
Brier scores reward calibration plus resolution — fence-sitting at 50% is calibrated cowardice, and every score needs a benchmark.
Foxes (many little things, "however") beat hedgehogs (One Big Idea, "certain") — and hedgehogs are worst inside their own specialty.
Fame predicts failure: TV rewards exactly the confident simplicity that ruins forecasts, because nobody keeps score.
The IARPA rout: scored amateurs beat rival teams by 30-70% and analysts with classified files by ~30% — measurement beats clearance.
Superforecasting is skill: ~70% stay in the top 2% year over year, seeing 300 days out as clearly as others see 100.
